source_pr.title: log-backup: use the span tree(instead of the naïve algorithm) to calculate the checkpoint
source_pr.body:
<!--

Thank you for contributing to TiDB!

PR Title Format:
1. pkg [, pkg2, pkg3]: what's changed
2. *: what's changed

-->

### What problem does this PR solve?
<!--

Please create an issue first to describe the problem.

There MUST be one line starting with "Issue Number:  " and 
linking the relevant issues via the "close" or "ref".

For more info, check https://pingcap.github.io/tidb-dev-guide/contribute-to-tidb/contribute-code.html#referring-to-an-issue.

-->

Issue Number: close #N/A

Problem Summary:
We were using a naive algorithm to advance the checkpoint before:
it simply maintains a mapping from `timestamp` to `ranges`. Which would probably store too many radiant items.

### What is changed and how it works?

This PR added a new data structure named `spans.ValueSorted` in the code.
It contains a non-overlapped, full range tree as the "primary key" and a binary search tree with `timestamp` as key `range` as value for indexing. 
The primary tree has some characteristics:
- FULL: The union of all ranges it contains is the full range (the universal set).
- NON-OVERLAPPED: Any pair of ranges from it doesn't overlap.

For keeping these characteristics, the interface it provides is limited, when we inserting some ranges into it, it would "merge" those new ranges into its current state. 

An example here:

Each flush would generate a new checkpoint: usually, it updates the checkpoint of some range by the regions it flushed. In a more detailed view, you can imagine the key space as a line:
```
|___________________________________________________________________________|
""                                                                        inf
Then the checkpoint c of each range can be presented as:
|___________________________________________________________________________|
^-----------------^-----------------^-----------------^---------------------^
|      c = 42     |      c = 43     |     c = 45      |      c = 41         |
```
Once querying a checkpoint of some range, we simply choose the minimal of all its intersected ranges.
```
|___________________________________________________________________________|
^-----------------^-----------------^-----------------^---------------------^
|      c = 42     |      c = 43     |     c = 45      |      c = 41         |
    ^--------------------------^
    |   c = min(42, 43) = 42   |
```
A flush would update the checkpoint of a subrange. At this time, we fill the points in the range with max(new_checkpoint, old_checkpoint), we are going to call this step Merge, just like:
```
|___________________________________________________________________________|
^-----------------^-----------------^-----------------^---------------------^
|      c = 42     |      c = 43     |     c = 45      |      c = 41         |
                       ^--------------------------^
                       |      merge(c = 44)       |
```
Would Give:
```
|___________________________________________________________________________|
^-----------------^----^------------^-------------^---^---------------------^
|      c = 42     | 43 |   c = 44   |     c = 45      |      c = 41         |
                                    |-------------|
                                    Unchanged, because 44 < 45.
```

### Check List

Tests <!-- At least one of them must be included. -->

- [x] Unit test
- [] Integration test
- [ ] Manual test (add detailed scripts or steps below)
- [ ] No code


### Release note

<!-- compatibility change, improvement, bugfix, and new feature need a release note -->

Please refer to [Release Notes Language Style Guide](https://pingcap.github.io/tidb-dev-guide/contribute-to-tidb/release-notes-style-guide.html) to write a quality release note.

```release-note
None
```

root_cause_explanation: The test was flaky due to concurrency races or unsynchronized shared state. Asynchronous state transitions need polling/Eventually-style checks. This PR stabilizes it by: log-backup: use the span tree(instead of the naïve algorithm) to calculate the checkpoint.
fix_pattern: log-backup: use the span tree(instead of the naïve algorithm) to calculate the checkpoint

evidence: files: br/pkg/logutil/logging.go, br/pkg/restore/BUILD.bazel, br/pkg/storage/BUILD.bazel, br/pkg/streamhelper/BUILD.bazel, br/pkg/streamhelper/advancer.go, br/pkg/streamhelper/basic_lib_for_test.go, br/pkg/streamhelper/regioniter_test.go, br/pkg/streamhelper/spans/BUILD.bazel, br/pkg/streamhelper/spans/sorted.go, br/pkg/streamhelper/spans/sorted_test.go, br/pkg/streamhelper/spans/utils.go, br/pkg/streamhelper/spans/utils_test.go, br/pkg/streamhelper/spans/value_sorted.go, br/pkg/streamhelper/spans/value_sorted_test.go, br/pkg/streamhelper/tsheap.go, br/pkg/streamhelper/tsheap_test.go, br/pkg/utils/iter/BUILD.bazel, br/pkg/utils/key.go
